insights: All five user agents have exactly 100 incident assignments, indicating no variation in workload volume across agents and therefore no evidence of increasing or uneven assignment pressure.

The uniform distribution of assignments across category, location, and priority dimensions confirms that workload balance is maintained not only in volume but also in operational complexity and urgency profile.

The absence of any deviation from perfect balance—across agents, time (2023-01-02 to 2024-01-31), and key incident attributes—rules out workload-driven performance differentials, burnout risk, or service bottlenecks attributable to assignment growth.

Since incident volume per agent is static and fully controlled, observed agent-level outcomes (e.g., resolution time, satisfaction) are unlikely to stem from assignment quantity and should instead be investigated through non-volume factors such as incident difficulty, tooling, or knowledge gaps.
summary: The investigation finds no evidence supporting the premise that increasing incident assignments are impacting user agents: all five agents carry identical workloads (100 assignments each), with perfectly uniform distribution across category, location, and priority—indicating deliberate, stable, and equitable assignment governance. This null result eliminates assignment volume imbalance as a driver of agent performance variance, service risk, or operational strain during the analysis period. Subsequent analysis should pivot to non-quantity factors—such as incident complexity, resolution support systems, or skill-alignment gaps—to explain any observed differences in agent outcomes.
